summary: Gruber details his switch from Dropbox's bloated official Mac client to Maestral, a lightweight open-source alternative that provides simple folder syncing with minimal UI. After 11 months of satisfaction, he encountered a bug where Maestral crashes silently during sleep. He walks through two workarounds: using LaunchControl to create a launchd job that automatically relaunches Maestral, and using Keyboard Maestro with AppleScript to monitor and relaunch while logging crash frequency. The post is a classic Mac power-user tutorial, complete with an AppleScript compilation quirk explanation.
key_points: Dropbox remains a great service but its official Mac client has become bloated and confusing, while Maestral offers what Dropbox's client once was: a simple syncing folder with a menu bar interface
Dropbox's transition to Apple's File Provider APIs forces cloud-only storage in ~/Library/CloudStorage/, which Maestral avoids by letting users place their Dropbox folder anywhere
LaunchControl provides a simple GUI for creating launchd jobs that can keep any app alive automatically after crashes
An AppleScript quirk means you can't use 'tell application' blocks to check if an app is running, because compilation itself launches the targeted app, defeating the check
money_quotes: Dropbox remains an excellent service, seemingly utterly reliable, with fair pricing.
Dropbox's official Mac client has been a confusing, ugly, resource-heavy mess for years, and is only getting worse.
It provides exactly what I want in a Mac Dropbox client, and what the official Dropbox app once was, long ago: a folder that syncs, with a minimal user interface that lives in a menu bar app.
The whole point of Dropbox is that it just works in the background, automatically.
The obvious band-aid solution is to manually relaunch Maestral each time I wake my Mac, but that's annoying and easy to forget.
Which, to be a HIG pedant, ought to be title-cased: 'For Current User'.
key_insight: When a lightweight tool solves a problem better than the official bloated alternative, it's worth the trade-off of occasionally needing to work around rough edges with other power-user tools.
